stewart_read: Glenn volunteers that he removed 69 trees — a significant investment that signals he has already been thinking about solar seriously enough to prepare his property for it. Meg responds with a brief acknowledgment ('full sun exposure') and moves on. This is the single strongest qualification signal on the call and it passed without being anchored.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have noted it internally and planned to reference it in the design handoff — but there is no evidence she tied it back to Glenn's motivation or used it to deepen the interest_in_solar verify item.
coaching_implication: Ask Meg: what does it mean that a customer cleared 69 trees before you called? That's not a casual homeowner — that's someone who has already committed time and money to making solar viable. The manager should use this moment to show Meg how to anchor an enthusiasm signal: 'Glenn, you cleared 69 trees — that tells me you've been thinking about this for a while. What got you started down that road?' That question opens the interest_in_solar verify item and deepens emotional investment simultaneously.

quote: that's a 40 by 60 with the majority of that roof wide open to the sky.
stewart_read: Glenn describes a 40x60 detached garage with a largely unobstructed roof — a second viable installation surface beyond the main house. Meg acknowledges it ('40 by 60. Yeah. I mean, it looks great') but does not flag it as a separate design consideration or note it for the closer. The codex's TBD solar_for_secondary_structure cross-sell signal applies here. Glenn's garage roof is already open to the sky; this is a handoff-specificity gap, not a roof-replacement cross-sell. The closer needs to know this surface exists and may warrant its own system sizing conversation.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg's line 'I'll send that to my team just so they're aware' (07:33) could be read as implicitly routing the garage detail — but there is no explicit flag that the garage is a second installation candidate, and the closer has no way to know its significance from a generic team note.
coaching_implication: Coach Meg to explicitly name secondary structures in her handoff note: 'Glenn has a 40x60 detached garage with an open south-facing roof — closer should ask whether Glenn wants a design that includes the garage as a second array.' This is a handoff discipline issue, not a setter cross-sell miss. The manager should also flag this for the codex TBD solar_for_secondary_structure entry.

quote: they built a giant solar field two miles south of my house here. And ever since that ever since that went online, my electric skyrocketed.
stewart_read: Glenn's bill story is unusually specific: his bill doubled-to-tripled with no change in usage, and he has a concrete theory (the nearby solar field). Meg responds with 'Man, okay. That's super weird' and immediately pivots to the credit qualifier. The bill was captured but not flipped. The codex's bill_anchoring section is explicit: 'capture alone is not enough — the bill must be FLIPPED into emotional alignment-against-the-utility.' The flip was available and unused.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have judged that Glenn was already emotionally aligned (he volunteered the story unprompted) and that pressing further would feel redundant. But the codex's objection_inversion mechanic is not about creating alignment that doesn't exist — it's about naming and amplifying alignment that does, so it becomes the emotional engine of the appointment.
coaching_implication: This is the highest-leverage missed move on the call. Show Meg the flip template from coaching_philosophy.objection_inversion and ask her to construct a version using Glenn's own words: his bill went from $300 to $600–$1,100 with no usage change, and he has a specific grievance. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): 'Glenn, you're paying double what you were paying two years ago and your usage hasn't changed — that's not your bill, that's AES charging you whatever they want. That's exactly the problem solar fixes.' The number Glenn gave ($600–$1,100) is the leverage; Meg never used it.

quote: I just had my jaw replaced on that at work.
stewart_read: Glenn's jaw surgery disclosure opens a thread that leads to his full story: disabled vet, 31 surgeries over 20+ years, built a 30-semi trucking business while technically disabled. Meg's empathy responses ('Oh my gosh,' 'thank you for your service,' 'That's incredible') are genuine and appropriate — the empathy was present. The gap is the missing value bridge. The codex's empathy_on_vulnerability_disclosure section specifies the move as 'empathy + bridge to value' — the empathy landed, the bridge did not. Glenn's fixed-income-adjacent context (disabled vet, medical costs, bill spike) makes the solar value proposition directly relevant to his situation, and Meg never connected the dots.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have judged that pivoting to a value bridge immediately after Glenn's service disclosure would feel transactional. That instinct is understandable — but the bridge does not have to be immediate. It could have come after the rapport moment settled, before the bill collection pivot.
coaching_implication: Coach Meg on the distinction between empathy and the empathy-plus-bridge move. The empathy was right. The bridge was missing. A suggested rephrase (intent, not verbatim): after Glenn's story settles, something like — 'Glenn, with everything you've been through, the last thing you should be dealing with is a bill that doubled for no reason. That's exactly what we're here to fix.' This connects his personal narrative to the product's value without fabricating numbers or feeling transactional.

quote: What we'll do here is when we build a design, I'll send that to my team just so they're aware and can see exactly how things are looking.
stewart_read: By this point in the call, Meg has confirmed address and utility company — approximately 2 of 8 required verify items. She never asked about title/co-owners, residence length, interest in solar (beyond the tree-clearing signal she did not probe), roof type/age, or prior designs. The codex sets a 6-of-8 completion threshold. The call ends with roughly 3 of 8 items captured (address, utility, credit score). The missing items carry real downstream risk: no co-decision-maker status means the closer walks in blind on spouse/title questions; no prior designs means no competitive context; no roof age means no DQ check.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have had prior lead data covering some verify items (recycled lead scenario per context.lead_types_and_bill_collection_rules). Without lead-type metadata, Stewart cannot confirm this. If this is a paid lead with no prior data, the verify gap is a structural miss.
coaching_implication: Pull the verify checklist with Meg item by item. The five missing items are not optional conversation — they are qualification gates. Specifically: (1) title/co-owners is the most urgent miss because it determines whether the Monday appointment is structurally sound; (2) prior designs would have surfaced competitive context the closer needs. The manager should ask Meg to walk through how she would have woven these into the conversation naturally — the codex's framing is 'qualification disguised as rapport,' not a checkbox interrogation.

quote: Let's do 9AM so that that'll wait because I dropped my daughter off at school. I usually head to work then, but I can just come back here and hang out.
stewart_read: Glenn's own scheduling logic reveals the appointment is sandwiched between school drop-off and his departure to the office. He plans to 'come back here and hang out' — which implies he may be at home briefly before leaving. Meg does not set the computer/no-driving anchor, does not confirm Glenn will be at a computer, and does not deliver the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. The codex marks both as required button-up elements with explicit no-show risk warnings. Glenn's words make the risk concrete, not hypothetical.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have assumed Glenn would be home and available since he volunteered the time slot. But Glenn's framing ('I can just come back here and hang out') suggests the appointment is a detour from his normal Monday routine, not a committed block — which makes the anchor more important, not less.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the button_up checklist and play back Glenn's 16:28 line. Ask: 'If Glenn drops his daughter off, comes home, and the closer calls at 9AM — is Glenn at a computer or is he already thinking about getting to the office?' The no-driving/computer anchor and the 'is that fair?' handshake are the tools that convert a tentative slot into a committed sit. Both were skipped here.

quote: if you need anything from me, Glenn, you've got my phone number. You can call me or text me. It should come directly to me, and I can help you out.
stewart_read: Meg closes by giving Glenn her personal phone number as the follow-up channel. The codex's bill_collection.stewart_automated_bill_capture section flags this pattern explicitly: 'Setters give out personal phone numbers for bill follow-up... Privacy + scale exposure.' In this case the bill was already captured, so the personal number offer is a rapport gesture rather than a bill-collection workaround — but it still establishes a personal channel that bypasses Ion's systems and creates the same privacy/scale exposure the codex flags.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may be using the personal number offer as a trust-building micro-trade with a customer she has built genuine rapport with. That instinct is not wrong — the codex's micro_trade pattern recognizes personal commitments as high-leverage on suspicious customers. Glenn is not suspicious, but the gesture does reinforce the relationship.
coaching_implication: This is a lower-priority coaching note. The manager should acknowledge that Meg's instinct (personal accessibility = trust) is directionally right, then introduce the codex's concern about personal number exposure at scale. The conversation is: 'What happens when you have 20 Glenns and they all have your personal number?' This is a systems conversation, not a rep-failure conversation.

quote: Way too far. I used to have a $300 a month average bill, but in the last six months, it's averaging anywhere I'm paying anywhere from 600 to $1,100 a month for nothing changing.
stewart_read: Glenn's bill range ($600–$1,100/month) is the emotional core of this call. He volunteers the full context — the prior baseline, the spike, the duration, and his theory — without being prompted beyond the average-bill question. This is a customer who has been sitting with bill pain and has a story ready. Meg's summary at 08:21 ('So about an $800 a month average') is a rep inference, not Glenn's framing. The closer should know Glenn's actual stated range is $600–$1,100, not a flat $800 average.
coaching_implication: Two separate coaching points here. First, for Meg: when a customer gives a range, do not flatten it to an average — the range itself is the emotional data. '$600 to $1,100 with nothing changing' is more powerful than '$800 on average.' Second, for the handoff brief: the closer must know Glenn's stated range, not Meg's inference. The $800 figure is Meg's math; Glenn's words are $600–$1,100.
